analysis_summary: The proposed method produced 7 finding(s) from 8 analysis obligation(s).

findings.summary: Potential unauthorized debug access to critical system control registers if debug bus can target the soc_ctrl address range.
findings.vulnerability_category: write / apb_soc_ctrl registers (e.g., boot addresses, cluster control, clock selects) / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: Master on s_apb_debug_bus (debug interface) Operation: write Object: apb_soc_ctrl registers (e.g., boot addresses, cluster control, clock selects) Expected guard: debug_authorized state or a lock bit preventing writes when debug is not authenticated Observed behavior: apb_soc_ctrl register writes are enabled solely on PSEL && PENABLE && PWRITE with no debug authorization check; REG_FCBOOT, REG_JTAGREG, REG_CLUSTER_CTRL, etc. are directly written. Path: s_apb_debug_bus → APB node (periph_bus_wrap) → soc_ctrl_master → apb_soc_ctrl (needs confirmation of bus connectivity) Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Unauthorized debug master could hijack boot address, cluster control, clock selects, leading to code execution or denial of service.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Exact connection of s_apb_debug_bus to the APB peripheral bus not fully traced; could be that debug bus is a separate master that only accesses debug region. If debug bus cannot reach soc_ctrl range, this vulnerability may not be exploitable.

findings.vulnerability_category: write / configure / Internal registers including r_jtag_rego (REG_JTAGREG), fc_bootaddr, etc. / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: JTAG TAP via soc_jtag_reg_i Operation: write / configure Object: Internal registers including r_jtag_rego (REG_JTAGREG), fc_bootaddr, etc. Expected guard: JTAG authentication or lock bit gating JTAG-driven writes Observed behavior: REG_JTAGREG (0x74) can be written directly via APB when PSEL && PENABLE && PWRITE; no JTAG auth check. soc_jtag_reg_i is synchronized but not used for write gating. Path: soc_jtag_reg_i → synchronization registers → r_jtag_rego write via APB case REG_JTAGREG Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: JTAG could write to SOC control registers via the APB interface without authentication.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Need to confirm if JTAG interface can drive PADDR/PWDATA to reach this register, but the register exists and lacks protection.

findings.summary: Missing lock bit to protect FLL configuration.
findings.vulnerability_category: write / FLL control registers in apb_fll_if / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: Any APB master (CPU, debug, DMA) Operation: write Object: FLL control registers in apb_fll_if Expected guard: A lock bit that, once set, prevents further writes until a reset or a special unlock sequence Observed behavior: apb_fll_if does not contain a software lock bit for write protection. Write access (fll1_wr_access, etc.) is enabled whenever PSEL && PENABLE && PWRITE and address match; the fll_lock inputs are used only for status read, not for write gating. Path: APB write transaction → apb_fll_if → fll1_wr_access/fll2_wr_access/fll3_wr_acces → FLL read/write state machine → fll1_req/fll1_data etc. Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Unauthorized reconfiguration of FLL frequencies could cause clock instability or fault injection.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: FLL macros may have internal write protection, but not visible in this module.

findings.summary: No lock protection on boot address registers.
findings.vulnerability_category: write / fc_bootaddr_o and cluster_boot_addr_o registers in apb_soc_ctrl / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: Any APB master (CPU, debug, DMA) Operation: write Object: fc_bootaddr_o and cluster_boot_addr_o registers in apb_soc_ctrl Expected guard: A lock bit or privilege check preventing modification after secure boot, or restricted to a trusted master Observed behavior: REG_FCBOOT (0x04) writes directly to r_bootaddr; REG_CLUSTER_BOOT_ADDR0/1 write to r_cluster_boot. No lock or lifecycle gating; write enable solely from PSEL && PENABLE && PWRITE. Path: APB write → apb_soc_ctrl → case REG_FCBOOT/REG_CLUSTER_BOOT_ADDR → r_bootaddr/r_cluster_boot Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Boot address hijacking could redirect execution to malicious code.

findings.vulnerability_category: read / write / TCDM or other internal memory regions / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: Debug interface (lint_debug, lint_jtag) via XBAR_TCDM_BUS Operation: read / write Object: TCDM or other internal memory regions Expected guard: Debug authentication state that blocks memory accesses when not authorized Observed behavior: soc_interconnect_wrap instantiates debug bus directly as DBG_RX with no authentication gating; transactions are passed to memory controllers unconditionally. Path: s_lint_debug_bus/s_lint_jtag_bus → soc_interconnect_wrap → soc_interconnect → memory banks Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Debugger can read sensitive data from TCDM/SRAM and inject code.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Not reviewed soc_interconnect internal logic, but from wrapper it appears there is no authentication gate.

findings.summary: Potential unrestricted DMA access; requires further analysis.
findings.vulnerability_category: read / write / Any memory region, especially protected assets / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: udma_subsystem (DMA) Operation: read / write Object: Any memory region, especially protected assets Expected guard: PMP-like range checks or access control configuration that limits DMA accesses to allowed memory ranges Observed behavior: udma_subsystem has master ports (l2_tx_master, l2_rx_master) connected to interconnect; no external configuration ports for address range limits observed in the instantiation. Path: udma_subsystem → l2_tx_master/l2_rx_master → soc_interconnect_wrap → soc_interconnect → L2 memory Closure verdict: Related RTL evidence exists, but some core evidence slots remain incomplete.
findings.security_impact: Unrestricted DMA could read/modify protected memory.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Internal RTL of udma_subsystem.sv not inspected; need to check for address filtering logic.

findings.summary: GPIO lock is present but can be cleared by the same APB master, limiting its effectiveness.
findings.vulnerability_category: write / gpio_dir, gpio_out registers in apb_gpio / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: Any APB master Operation: write Object: gpio_dir, gpio_out registers in apb_gpio Expected guard: A lock bit or privilege check that prevents unauthorized GPIO configuration Observed behavior: apb_gpio contains r_gpio_lock register with bits 0 and 2 protecting writes to PADDIR and PADOUT respectively. However, the lock register itself appears writable without restriction; an attacker can simply clear the lock bits to re-enable writes. Path: APB write → apb_gpio → case REG_PADDIR/REG_PADOUT with conditional pwdata_l based on r_gpio_lock bits; but lock can be disabled via REG_GPIOLOCK. Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: GPIO direction/output can be subverted if attacker gains APB write access, bypassing the lock.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Complete write access to REG_GPIOLOCK not shown; assume it is writable without protection.

global_uncertainty: No closure-level missing evidence was recorded beyond the visible source scope limitations.
